planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-drained soil; scarify or nick seeds to improve germination rates. Inoculate with appropriate Bradyrhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation. Mow or incorporate into soil before seed set to prevent prolific self-seeding.

directions: An upright warm-season annual legume naturalized throughout the southeastern US and tropical regions worldwide, producing trifoliate leaves and tall racemes of bright yellow, pea-like flowers from late summer through fall, followed by inflated seed pods that rattle audibly when ripe. Scarify or nick seeds before direct sowing after last frost to speed germination; inoculate with Bradyrhizobium for best nitrogen-fixation results in new planting sites. As a nitrogen-fixing cover crop and green manure, it builds soil fertility rapidly in warm, well-drained, full-sun beds in zones 8-11. Mow or till under before seed set — all parts, especially the seeds, contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids that are toxic to livestock and humans if ingested.

planting.note: Direct sow at 20-30 lb/acre (or broadcast thickly) after last frost on warm, moist, well-drained soil; scarify seeds to hasten germination. Incorporate as green manure before seed set to prevent self-seeding and avoid seed toxicity risks to livestock.

directions: A tropical annual legume with distinctive wedge-shaped, retuse-tipped simple leaves and upright racemes of bright yellow pea flowers from late summer through fall; native to tropical Asia and Africa, it is one of the most widely grown cover crop legumes in subtropical and tropical agriculture globally. Direct sow after last frost at high seeding rates on warm, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; scarify seeds to improve germination and inoculate with Bradyrhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation. It establishes rapidly and suppresses weeds effectively, making it a productive green manure in Hawaii, Florida, and tropical farming systems. Incorporate into the soil before seed set, as all plant parts — especially the seeds — are toxic to livestock and humans due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid content.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; no scarification is typically necessary but nicking hard seeds speeds germination. Incorporate as green manure before seed set to prevent self-seeding and protect livestock.

directions: A tropical annual legume covered in soft, spreading hairs, with trifoliate leaves and short racemes of small yellow pea flowers followed by distinctively woolly, inflated seed pods that rattle when ripe; naturalized widely across the southeastern US coastal plain in disturbed, open, moist to dry ground. Direct sow after last frost in warm, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil — it tolerates low-fertility sandy soils well and fixes nitrogen through Rhizobium symbiosis. As a warm-season cover crop in tropical and subtropical gardens and fields, it establishes readily from seed with little input and builds organic matter effectively when tilled under as green manure. Like all Crotalaria species, the seeds contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock and humans — always incorporate before seed set and keep animals away from flowering or seeding plants.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; scarify seeds lightly for more uniform germination. Inoculate with Aeschynomene-specific Bradyrhizobium inoculant for nitrogen fixation in soils where the plant has not grown before.

directions: A native annual legume of moist, open, disturbed habitats, roadsides, and wet meadows in the southeastern US and throughout tropical America, producing delicate, sensitive pinnate leaves and clusters of small yellow, brownish-red-veined pea flowers from summer through fall. The jointed seed pods break apart at the segments at maturity — a characteristic trait that gives the plant the common name 'joint-vetch' — and the seeds are relished by mourning doves, quail, and other seed-eating birds, making this an excellent wildlife food plot legume for warm climates. Direct sow after last frost in moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; inoculate with Aeschynomene-specific Bradyrhizobium to maximize nitrogen fixation in new planting sites. It tolerates periodic flooding and is well adapted to the wet, disturbed margins of ponds and seasonal wet areas throughout zones 7-11.
sources: USDA NRCS Plant Guide: Aeschynomene americana
National Wildlife Federation Native Plant Finder

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; tolerates saturated and periodically flooded conditions far better than most legumes. Inoculate with Aeschynomene-specific Bradyrhizobium; stem nodules form naturally in waterlogged conditions without inoculant in established paddy systems.

directions: A pantropical annual legume native to India and widely naturalized in the southeastern US and throughout tropical regions, recognized by its sensitive pinnate leaves that fold when touched or shaded — a trait that earns it the common name 'shyleaf' — and small, yellow, red-veined pea flowers that appear from summer through fall. Unlike most legumes, it forms nitrogen-fixing nodules on its stems as well as its roots, and these stem nodules function effectively even in flooded, waterlogged paddy soils, making it a foundational nitrogen-fixing component of traditional Asian and African rice-based farming systems. Direct sow after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun, and inoculate with Aeschynomene-specific Bradyrhizobium in new sites; it establishes readily in poorly drained areas where other cover crop legumes fail. An excellent nitrogen-fixing cover crop for low, wet areas, rain gardens, and flooded margins in zones 8-11.
